Q1: My study routine was honestly chaotic but intense. I'm 29, working, and juggling a lot, so I couldn't afford the luxury of consistent daily study blocks. I'd usually batch my studying into marathon sessions—maybe two or three times a week for several hours each. I relied heavily on listening to lectures during my commute and while doing chores, which is how I absorbed most of the content. When exams approached, everything else in my life basically stopped and I went into full crisis mode.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what was happening in my life. Some weeks I barely touched anything because work was demanding or I was dealing with personal stuff. Then when deadlines loomed or exams approached, I'd suddenly become hyper-focused and put in 20+ hours in just a few days. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I survived. The regularity just wasn't possible given my circumstances.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being honest. I'd log in when I realized something was due soon or when I needed to check if I'd missed an announcement. Sometimes I'd get a notification from a peer in our support group asking about something, and that would prompt me to check in. I also logged in specifically to download audio files or lecture recordings that I could listen to later offline.
Q4: Not really a daily routine, but the peer support group we had made me check in more regularly than I would have otherwise. When my study buddies were active, I felt more accountable. There were stretches where I'd log in every couple of days just to see what others were discussing. But calling it a 'normal routine' would be generous—it was more like a sporadic habit driven by social obligation and anxiety.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I learn best by listening. I'd often play them at 1.5x speed while doing other things, then replay sections I didn't catch. The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful too—seeing how my peers interpreted concepts helped things click. When the peer support intervention connected me with other students, that human element made the content feel more accessible and less isolating.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and written resources. With my auditory learning style, staring at dense text just doesn't work for me—I zone out and retain nothing. I also ignored a lot of the optional practice quizzes because I was always in triage mode, focusing only on what was absolutely necessary. If it wasn't going to be graded or directly tested, it often fell to the bottom of my priority list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to exam performance or if it helped clarify something I was struggling with. Given my limited resources and time, I had to be strategic. If something could be listened to rather than read, that was a huge plus. Activities that my peer group recommended also felt more worthwhile because I trusted their judgment.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're anxious about passing and have limited time, you focus on survival—the required stuff. Required activities had weight and consequences, so they got my attention. Optional things, even if valuable, always got pushed aside because there was always something more urgent demanding my energy.
Q9: Absolutely, this was actually my primary mode of studying. I'd download lecture audio files and listen to them repeatedly on my phone—during commutes, while cooking, even while trying to fall asleep. I also recorded myself summarizing key concepts and played those back. My actual learning happened mostly offline, away from the LMS, in stolen moments throughout my day.
Q10: Most of my studying wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd download a lecture once, but then listen to it five or six times over the following week—none of that repeated engagement would register. I also studied with my peer support group in person or over phone calls, discussing concepts verbally. My exam score reflects learning that happened entirely outside the platform's visibility.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video lecture once to download it, then spend days working through that material offline. The LMS would show one brief login, but the actual learning stretched across multiple sessions over several days. This was especially true for complex topics where I needed to hear explanations multiple times to really understand them.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods. My anxiety would spike, and I'd feel paralyzed—knowing I needed to engage but feeling overwhelmed by how behind I was. Financial stress from my low resources situation made it hard to focus. Sometimes work would explode and I'd go a week without touching anything academic, then face the mountain of catch-up with dread. The peer support helped pull me back during those moments.
Q13: Constantly. Fatigue was a big one—after long work days, my brain just couldn't process new information effectively. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS with good intentions but end up just staring at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I was always making impossible choices about what got my attention. The LMS often lost to more immediate life demands.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the lecture playing but my mind would be elsewhere—worrying about bills, thinking about work problems, or just mentally checked out from exhaustion. I'd technically watch the whole video but couldn't tell you what was said. Those sessions felt like going through the motions without actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made it worse—the bigger the task felt, the more I avoided it. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was fresher, but tomorrow always had its own problems. My assignment completion rate was terrible because of this avoidance pattern, even though I knew the material well enough to ace exams.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my savior. They created intense stress and anxiety, but they also forced action. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift and procrastinate. With one approaching, I'd suddenly become incredibly focused and productive. My LMS usage probably spiked dramatically in the 48 hours before anything was due, then dropped to almost nothing afterward.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Positive feedback from peers in our support group motivated me to keep going—knowing others were struggling too made me feel less alone. But formal feedback on the few assignments I submitted sometimes increased my anxiety rather than helping. I'd obsess over criticism and it would make me more hesitant to submit the next thing.
Q18: The course structure was challenging for someone in my situation. The steady drip of assignments assumed consistent availability that I just didn't have. However, having recorded lectures I could access anytime was a lifesaver. The peer support intervention was the structural element that helped most—having designated people to connect with gave me accountability and emotional support when the course design itself felt overwhelming.
Q19: The flexibility of asynchronous content made engagement possible for me—without that, I couldn't have participated at all. But the assignment schedule was brutal for someone balancing work and limited resources. More audio-based content options would have helped my learning style. The peer support pairing was genuinely helpful; without those connections, I might have dropped out during my worst anxiety periods.
Q20: That describes me somewhat. I was highly engaged in consuming content—watching videos, reading discussions, downloading materials—but my assignment submission rate was abysmal. For me, it was anxiety and perfectionism. I'd learn the material but freeze when it came to producing work for evaluation. The gap between understanding content and submitting polished assignments felt insurmountable sometimes.
Q21: That's essentially my story. My LMS activity was relatively low because I did most of my learning offline, yet I earned a distinction. The exam score shows I mastered the material—I just did it through repeated audio listening and peer discussions that happened outside the system. Low login numbers don't mean low learning; they might just mean different learning strategies.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone accessed materials and how long they were connected, but they completely miss the quality of that engagement. They can't see me listening to a downloaded lecture for the fifth time while washing dishes. They can't capture the 'aha' moment I had discussing a concept with my peer support partner over coffee. Logs show presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means the material is actively occupying my mind—I'm wrestling with concepts, making connections, questioning ideas. It's not about screen time or login frequency. For me, engagement often looked like talking through problems out loud, explaining concepts to myself while walking, or suddenly understanding something while doing something completely unrelated. It's a mental state, not a measurable behavior.
Q24: The LMS would never show the emotional journey—the anxiety attacks before logging in, the relief when a peer reached out, the pride when something finally clicked. It wouldn't capture my resilience in returning after periods of complete disengagement, or the peer conversations that kept me from giving up. My story is about surviving and succeeding despite circumstances, and data points can't tell that story.
